summary: Italian Renaissance composer (c. 1525–1594) from the town of Palestrina, near Rome. Master of Renaissance polyphony and counterpoint. His style, emphasizing stepwise motion, dissonances on weak beats, and smooth voice leading, became the model for Renaissance sacred music. Apocryphally credited with saving polyphony from being banned at the Council of Trent by composing the Pope Marcellus Mass. His compositional style was codified by Johann Joseph Fux in the influential counterpoint treatise Gradus ad Parnassum.

works.name: Pope Marcellus Mass (Missa Papae Marcelli)
works.description: Palestrina's most famous work, a missa sine nomine (mass without a name) for six voices. All movements except the Agnus Dei are scored for six voices, and a two-part Agnus Dei adds a seventh voice. Apocryphally credited with convincing the Council of Trent not to ban polyphonic music from worship. A mass by this name was also arranged for orchestra by J. S. Bach. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Apocryphally saved polyphony at / convinced the Council of Trent not to ban polyphony
examples: This composer's most famous missa sine nomine apocryphally 'saved polyphony' at the Council of Trent.
According to legend, this composer convinced the Council of Trent not to ban polyphonic music from worship.

clues.clue: All movements except the Agnus Dei scored for six voices; second Agnus Dei adds a seventh voice with ascending fourth head-motif
examples: The head-motif of an ascending fourth followed by a stepwise descent appears in a piece by this composer that adds a seventh voice in its second Agnus Dei movement.
All but the Agnus Dei of this composer's most famous mass are scored for six voices.

clues.clue: A missa sine nomine (mass without a name)
examples: This composer wrote a 'Mass With No Name'.
A mass by this composer 'without a name' was arranged for orchestra by J. S. Bach.

clues.clue: The subject of a Hans Pfitzner opera — Pfitzner's opera is titled 'Palestrina'
examples: In an opera about this composer, he initially refuses to take a commission from Cardinal Borromeo, who then imprisons him.
This man celebrated in a Hans Pfitzner opera wrote a work for six voices that has a two-part Agnus Dei with intelligible words.

clues.clue: Words intelligible to parishioners — written to ensure listeners could understand the words
examples: Hans Pfitzner wrote an opera about a mass by this composer written to ensure that listeners could understand the words.
He wrote that work to convince the Council of Trent not to ban polyphonic music.

clues.clue: The Kyrie introduces the recurring motif D–D, D–G–F–E–D
examples: This man titles a mass whose Kyrie section introduces the following recurring motif: D [pause] D, D [pause] G, F, E, D.
The Kyrie of this mass by Giovanni Palestrina introduces the motif D D, D G F E D.

clues.clue: Named for a pope (Marcellus II) who reigned for only three weeks
examples: A session of the Council of Trent was legendarily persuaded by this composition to keep polyphony; it is named for a pontiff with a three-week reign.
This work is named for Pope Marcellus II, who had an extremely brief reign.

clues.clue: The Tallis Scholars paired it with Allegri's Miserere in their debut recording
examples: In their debut recording, the Tallis Scholars performed this ordinary mass alongside Allegri's Miserere.
A famous recording pairs Palestrina's missa sine nomine with Allegri's Miserere.

clues.clue: May have influenced Bach's Mass in B Minor
examples: A Missa sine nomine by this composer may have influenced Bach's Mass in B Minor.

works.name: Gradus ad Parnassum / Counterpoint Style
works.description: Palestrina's compositional style became the basis for Johann Joseph Fux's seminal counterpoint treatise Gradus ad Parnassum (1725). In the treatise, the character Aloysius represents Palestrina and teaches the student Josephus (representing Fux). Key principles: leaps countered by stepwise movement in the opposite direction; dissonances on weak beats only; smooth voice leading. Fux introduced the term 'species counterpoint' in the context of imitating Palestrina's style. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Palestrina's style codified in Johann Joseph Fux's Gradus ad Parnassum
examples: In a description of this composer's style, leaps are countered by stepwise movement in the other direction and dissonances fall on weak beats, as taught in Joseph Fux's Gradus ad Parnassum.
This composer's work was the basis of Johann Fux's textbook Gradus ad Parnassum.

clues.clue: Character Aloysius in Gradus represents Palestrina; student is Josephus (representing Fux)
examples: One treatise on counterpoint is presented as a dialogue between Aloysius, who represents this composer, and the student Josephus.
The master Aloysius teaches principles based on this composer's style in Johann Joseph Fux's Gradus ad Parnassum.

clues.clue: Style principles: dissonances on weak beats; leaps countered by stepwise movement; small intervals
examples: His most influential ideas were that dissonances should only be on passing notes and weak beats, music should have small leaps between notes counterbalanced by stepwise movement in the opposite direction.
The position that dissonance should normally fall on weak beats is derived from this composer's works in Johann Fux's Gradus ad Parnassum.

clues.clue: Term 'species counterpoint' introduced in reference to imitating this composer's style
examples: The term 'species counterpoint' was introduced in reference to attempts to imitate this composer's style.

clues.clue: Knud Jeppesen analyzed the dissonance treatment of this composer
examples: Knud Jeppesen analyzed the dissonance of a composer with this first name.

works.name: Song of Solomon Motets (Canticum Canticorum)
works.description: A cycle of 29 motets by Palestrina based on excerpts from the Song of Solomon (also called Canticum canticorum / Song of Songs). Palestrina composed over 250 motets and 140 madrigals total. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A cycle of 29 motets based on the Song of Solomon / Song of Songs
examples: This composer wrote a cycle of 29 motets based on excerpts from the Song of Solomon.
This Italian Renaissance composer wrote a collection of motets setting texts from the Song of Solomon, his Canticum canticorum.

clues.clue: Composed over 250 motets and 140 madrigals total; claimed to 'blush and grieve' over his madrigals
examples: A composer with this first name composed over 250 motets and 140 madrigals.
Appointed by Julius III to direct the Cappella Giulia, this composer said 'I blush and grieve' over their four collections of secular madrigals.

works.name: O Magnum Mysterium (motet)
works.description: A six-part motet by Palestrina, setting the O Magnum Mysterium text. Features unisons, word painting, and a strong plagal cadence. Was the first setting of this text. A piece by this composer emphasizes the word 'dominum' through homorhythmic repetition before a triple-time Alleluja section. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Six-part motet with unisons, word painting, and strong plagal cadence; first setting of O Magnum Mysterium
examples: This composer featured unisons, word painting, and a strong plagal cadence in a six-part motet, the first setting of O Magnum Mysterium.
This composer of Sicut Cervus is visited by angels and the spirits of dead composers in a 'musical legend' composed in 1917.

clues.clue: Emphasizes the word 'dominum' through homorhythmic repetition before a triple-time Alleluja section
examples: A piece by this composer emphasizes the word 'dominum' through homorhythmic repetition before a triple-time Alleluja section.

works.name: L'homme arme Masses
works.description: Palestrina wrote two masses based on the secular cantus firmus melody L'homme arme (The Armed Man) — one for five voices and one for four voices. L'homme arme was used as a cantus firmus in many Renaissance masses. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Wrote two L'homme arme masses: one for five voices and one for four voices
examples: This composer wrote two different masses, one for five voices and one for four, based on 'L'homme arme.'
Seventy years after Josquin, this composer wrote his own 'L'Homme Arme' mass.

works.name: Stabat Mater
works.description: An eight-part motet for unaccompanied double chorus, possibly written for Pope Gregory XIV. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A motet scored for unaccompanied double chorus; may have been written for Pope Gregory XIV
examples: This composer's Stabat Mater, a motet scored for unaccompanied Double Chorus, may have been written for Pope Gregory XIV.
This Renaissance composer and master of counterpoint whose works include... an eight-part setting of the Stabat Mater.

works.name: Sicut Cervus (motet)
works.description: A motet based on Psalm 42, which compares the soul to a deer thirsting for water (Sicut cervus desiderat = 'As the deer longs'). Palestrina put long melismas on the word 'desiderat.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Based on Psalm 42; compares the soul to a deer thirsting for water; long melismas on the word 'desiderat'
examples: This composer put long melismas on the word desiderat in a motet based on Psalm 42, which compares the soul to a deer thirsting for water.

comprehensive_summary:
Giovanni Pierluigi da Palestrina (c. 1525–1594) was an Italian Renaissance composer from the town of Palestrina near Rome, and the central figure of the Roman School of polyphonic sacred music. He is most famous for the Pope Marcellus Mass (Missa Papae Marcelli), a missa sine nomine (mass without a name) scored for six voices — all movements except the Agnus Dei use six voices, while the second Agnus Dei adds a seventh voice and features the head-motif of an ascending fourth followed by a stepwise descent. The Kyrie introduces the recurring motif D–D, D–G–F–E–D. The mass is named for Pope Marcellus II, who reigned for only three weeks. According to apocryphal legend, this mass convinced the Council of Trent not to ban polyphonic music from Catholic worship by demonstrating that multiple voices could still render the text intelligible to parishioners. The story forms the basis of a 1917 opera titled Palestrina by Hans Pfitzner, in which the composer initially refuses a commission from Cardinal Borromeo and is subsequently imprisoned. A version of the mass was arranged for orchestra by J. S. Bach, and it may have influenced Bach's Mass in B Minor. The Tallis Scholars paired it with Allegri's Miserere in their debut recording.

Palestrina's compositional style — characterized by stepwise melodic motion countering leaps, dissonances placed only on weak beats, and smooth voice leading — became the canonical model for Renaissance counterpoint. Johann Joseph Fux codified these principles in his influential 1725 treatise Gradus ad Parnassum, presented as a dialogue between the student Josephus (representing Fux) and the master Aloysius (representing Palestrina). Fux coined the term 'species counterpoint' in reference to exercises designed to imitate Palestrina's style. Knud Jeppesen also wrote a scholarly analysis of Palestrina's dissonance treatment.

Among his many works, Palestrina composed over 250 motets and 140 madrigals — the latter of which he later claimed to 'blush and grieve' over. His Canticum canticorum is a cycle of 29 motets setting texts from the Song of Solomon. The motet O Magnum Mysterium, scored for six voices, features unisons, word painting, and a strong plagal cadence, and was the first polyphonic setting of that text; a related piece emphasizes the word 'dominum' through homorhythmic repetition before a triple-time Alleluja. His motet Sicut Cervus, based on Psalm 42 comparing the soul to a deer thirsting for water, features prominent melismas on the word 'desiderat.' The Stabat Mater is an eight-part motet for unaccompanied double chorus, possibly composed for Pope Gregory XIV. He also wrote two L'homme arme masses (one for four voices, one for five).

Biographically, Palestrina was appointed by Pope Julius III to direct the Cappella Giulia and later served in the papal choir until dismissed for being married. He resigned from the church of St. John Lateran — for which he had written Lamentations — after a dispute over feeding his choirboys, and he spent the last two decades of his career as choirmaster of the Cappella Giulia. He joined with Annibale Zoilo in an unsuccessful attempt to restore the plainsong, and his first publication (1554 book of masses) was modeled on that of Cristobal de Morales. His 19th-century revival in Germany was analyzed by James Garratt, and he was credited with producing the new 'gradual' promoted by the Cecilian Movement. The Sanctus from Anton Bruckner's Mass in E Minor quotes a theme by Palestrina. The Sixteen have released a series of albums dedicated to his music.
